flashcards.front: What are the two competing hypotheses in a test of significance?
flashcards.back: The Null Hypothesis (H₀), which is the default claim of 'no effect' or 'no difference,' and the Alternative Hypothesis (H₁ or Hₐ), which is the claim we are testing for, suggesting a significant effect or difference.

flashcards.front: Define the p-value in the context of hypothesis testing.
flashcards.back: The p-value is the probability of obtaining test results at least as extreme as the observed results, assuming that the null hypothesis is true. A small p-value (typically ≤ α) provides strong evidence against the null hypothesis.

The significance level (alpha, α) is the threshold probability for rejecting the null hypothesis. It is the maximum probability of making a Type I error (rejecting a true H₀) that the researcher is willing to accept. Common choices are 0.05 or 0.01.

flashcards.front: Differentiate between a Type I error and a Type II error.
flashcards.back: A Type I error occurs when we incorrectly reject a true null hypothesis (a false positive). A Type II error occurs when we incorrectly fail to reject a false null hypothesis (a false negative).

Statistical power is the probability that a test will correctly reject a false null hypothesis. It is the complement of the probability of a Type II error (Power = 1 - β). A high-powered test is more likely to detect an effect if one truly exists.
